content.text: Azure documentation is hosted at learn.microsoft.com/azure and covers every service through conceptual guides, quickstarts, tutorials, REST API reference, CLI reference, and SDK reference. Knowing how the documentation is structured, how to search it effectively, and how to access the underlying source helps you find accurate answers faster than any third-party resource.

attrs.alt: Azure documentation ecosystem — key portals and their focus areas

content.text: The Azure documentation is organised into four broad layers, each serving a different stage of learning and development.
Architecture, key concepts, when to use the service
Get something running in under 10 minutes
Every endpoint, parameter, and response schema
Coding against the raw REST API
Every az command with flags and examples
Class and method docs per language

The REST API reference is the authoritative source for every Azure service's API surface — more precise than the SDK docs because it shows the raw wire format, authentication scopes, and API version history.

Every Azure REST API call requires an api-version query parameter. Using a deprecated or unsupported version silently falls back to different behavior in some services. The documentation shows the list of stable and preview versions for each API.

import requests
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

def list_api_versions(
    resource_provider: str,   # e.g. "Microsoft.Web"
    resource_type: str,       # e.g. "sites"
    subscription_id: str,
) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Return all registered API versions for a resource type.
    Useful for confirming the latest stable version before coding.
    """
    credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
    token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token

    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscription_id}"
        f"/providers/{resource_provider}"
        f"?api-version=2021-04-01"
    )
    resp = requests.get(url, headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}, timeout=30)
    resp.raise_for_status()

    for rt in resp.json().get("resourceTypes", []):
        if rt["resourceType"].lower() == resource_type.lower():
            return {
                "resource_type": f"{resource_provider}/{resource_type}",
                "api_versions": rt.get("apiVersions", []),
                "default_api_version": rt.get("defaultApiVersion"),
            }
    return {}

versions = list_api_versions("Microsoft.Web", "sites", "your-subscription-id")
print(f"Latest: {versions['api_versions'][0] if versions['api_versions'] else 'unknown'}")
print(f"Default: {versions.get('default_api_version')}")
print(f"All stable: {[v for v in versions.get('api_versions', []) if 'preview' not in v]}")
Reading API Reference for Request/Response Shapes

REST API reference pages follow a consistent structure: a table of URI parameters, the request body schema with all properties and their types, and the response body schema with example JSON. The 'Definitions' section at the bottom shows nested object schemas.

# Verify a REST API call matches the documented schema with a dry-run GET
# Example: list App Service sites in a resource group
SUBSCRIPTION="your-sub-id"
RG="my-rg"
TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --query accessToken -o tsv)

curl -s \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$SUBSCRIPTION/resourceGroups/$RG/providers/Microsoft.Web/sites?api-version=2022-03-01" \
  | python3 -m json.tool | head -60

# Compare the output keys against the documented response schema
# Doc URL: learn.microsoft.com/rest/api/appservice/web-apps/list-by-resource-group
Every az command is documented with its required and optional parameters, accepted values, and working examples. The CLI reference is searchable and versioned per CLI release.

# --help flag shows all parameters with types and defaults
az webapp create --help

# More detailed contextual help with examples
az interactive  # launches an interactive REPL with tab completion and docs

# Find all commands for a service (useful for discovering capabilities)
az webapp --help

# Check the CLI version and installed extensions
az version --output table
az extension list --output table

# Update the CLI to get latest commands matching current API versions
az upgrade

Every az command corresponds to one or more REST API calls. The --debug flag shows the exact HTTP requests the CLI makes — indispensable for understanding which API version and endpoint a command uses.

# Show the underlying REST calls made by a CLI command
az webapp show \
  --name my-api \
  --resource-group my-rg \
  --debug 2>&1 | grep -E "(urllib3|msrest|Request|Response)" | head -30

# Output includes:
# urllib3.connectionpool - Starting new HTTPS connection: management.azure.com
# msrest.http_logger - Request URL: https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/.../sites/my-api?api-version=2022-03-01
# msrest.http_logger - Response status: 200

The Azure-Samples GitHub organisation hosts thousands of working code examples — complete applications, not code snippets. Each repository includes a README with deployment instructions and targets a specific use case.

# Clone and run an Azure Samples repository
# Example: Python FastAPI on Azure Container Apps
git clone https://github.com/Azure-Samples/containerapps-album-api.git
cd containerapps-album-api

# Most samples include a deploy.sh or azd (Azure Developer CLI) template
# Azure Developer CLI (azd) provides standardised provision + deploy
azd init  # if starting from scratch
azd up    # provision infra + deploy app in one command

# List available azd templates matching a keyword
azd template list --filter "python"

The Architecture Center provides reference architectures — validated end-to-end designs for common workload patterns. Each architecture includes a component diagram, deployment guide, scalability and reliability considerations, and cost estimate.
Front Door, App Service, SQL, Traffic Manager
Revenue-critical web app with < 1 min RTO
AKS, Container Registry, Service Bus, Key Vault
Event Hubs, Azure Functions, Data Lake, Synapse
AKS, Azure Firewall, Application Gateway, AAD
API Management, Azure Functions, Cosmos DB
Low-traffic or spiky API without persistent compute
Virtual WAN, Firewall, VPN Gateway, ExpressRoute

Architecture Center designs are tested and maintained by the Azure engineering team. Prefer them over blog posts or community guides when designing production systems — they reflect current best practices and Azure service capabilities.

All Azure documentation is open-source on GitHub under the MicrosoftDocs organisation. Corrections, clarifications, and additions can be submitted as pull requests — the 'Edit' button on every docs page links directly to the source file.

# Clone the Azure docs repository for a service
git clone https://github.com/MicrosoftDocs/azure-docs.git
cd azure-docs

# Documentation is in Markdown under articles/<service-name>/
ls articles/app-service/ | head -20

# Make a correction in a Markdown file
# Then submit a PR to MicrosoftDocs/azure-docs
git checkout -b fix/app-service-typo
# edit articles/app-service/quickstart-python.md
git add articles/app-service/quickstart-python.md
git commit -m "Fix: correct Python runtime version in quickstart"
git push origin fix/app-service-typo
# Open PR at github.com/MicrosoftDocs/azure-docs/compare
The Azure Developer CLI (azd) and Documentation

The Azure Developer CLI (azd) is a command-line tool that reads standardised azure.yaml template files to provision infrastructure and deploy applications in a single workflow. It is tightly integrated with the documentation — most Architecture Center reference deployments now ship as azd templates.
Template Structure and the azure.yaml File

# azure.yaml — defines the azd template structure
name: my-api-app
metadata:
  template: my-api-app@0.0.1-beta

# Infra provisioning: points to Bicep or Terraform files
infra:
  provider: bicep          # bicep | terraform
  path: infra/            # folder containing main.bicep
  module: main            # the root module name

# Application services to build and deploy
services:
  api:
    project: ./src/api    # path to app source
    language: py          # py | ts | java | dotnet | go
    host: appservice      # appservice | containerapp | function | aks

# Hooks for pre/post lifecycle events
hooks:
  preprovision:
    shell: sh
    run: ./scripts/validate-env.sh
  postdeploy:
    shell: sh
    run: ./scripts/run-smoke-tests.sh

# Full azd workflow — provision + deploy from template
azd auth login

# Initialise from an existing sample (clones the repo and sets up azure.yaml)
azd init --template Azure-Samples/todo-python-mongo-aca

# Set environment variables (stored per-environment, not committed)
azd env set AZURE_LOCATION eastus2
azd env set AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID your-subscription-id

# Provision Azure resources (runs Bicep/Terraform under the hood)
azd provision

# Build and deploy application code
azd deploy

# Or do both in one command
azd up

# Check deployment status
azd show

# Tear down resources when done
azd down --purge
Browsing azd Templates from the CLI

# List templates available from the official gallery
azd template list

# Filter by language
azd template list --filter "python"

# Show details for a specific template
azd template show Azure-Samples/todo-python-mongo-aca

# Output includes:
# Name: todo-python-mongo-aca
# Description: A complete ToDo application with FastAPI, React, and MongoDB on Azure Container Apps
# Languages: Python, TypeScript
# Azure services: Container Apps, Cosmos DB, Application Insights, Container Registry

When your team maintains internal runbooks or Infrastructure-as-Code that references specific Azure REST API versions or CLI command flags, CI checks can catch regressions when APIs evolve. The following pattern validates that your Bicep or Terraform files reference currently-supported API versions.

"""
Scan a directory of Bicep files and flag resource types using deprecated API versions.
Useful as a CI gate to prevent deploying with outdated resource configurations.
"""
import re, subprocess, json, sys, requests
from pathlib import Path
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

def get_latest_stable_api_version(
    resource_provider: str,
    resource_type: str,
    subscription_id: str,
    token: str,
) -> str | None:
    """Return the most recent stable (non-preview) API version for a resource type."""
    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscription_id}"
        f"/providers/{resource_provider}?api-version=2021-04-01"
    )
    resp = requests.get(url, headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}, timeout=30)
    if not resp.ok:
        return None
    for rt in resp.json().get("resourceTypes", []):
        if rt["resourceType"].lower() == resource_type.lower():
            stable = [v for v in rt.get("apiVersions", []) if "preview" not in v]
            return stable[0] if stable else None
    return None

# Pattern: find apiVersion in Bicep files
# e.g.  resource appService 'Microsoft.Web/sites@2020-06-01' = {
BICEP_API_RE = re.compile(r"resource \w+ '([^@]+)@(\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}(?:-preview)?)'")

def scan_bicep_files(directory: str, subscription_id: str) -> list[dict]:
    credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
    token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token
    issues = []

    for bicep_file in Path(directory).rglob("*.bicep"):
        content = bicep_file.read_text()
        for m in BICEP_API_RE.finditer(content):
            full_type = m.group(1)   # e.g. Microsoft.Web/sites
            used_version = m.group(2)
            parts = full_type.split("/", 1)
            if len(parts) != 2:
                continue
            provider, rtype = parts
            latest = get_latest_stable_api_version(provider, rtype, subscription_id, token)
            if latest and used_version < latest:
                issues.append({
                    "file": str(bicep_file),
                    "resource_type": full_type,
                    "used_version": used_version,
                    "latest_stable": latest,
                })

    return issues

issues = scan_bicep_files("./infra", "your-subscription-id")
if issues:
    print(f"Found {len(issues)} outdated API versions:")
    for i in issues:
        print(f"  {i['file']}: {i['resource_type']} uses {i['used_version']}, latest is {i['latest_stable']}")
    sys.exit(1)
else:
    print("All Bicep API versions are current.")

import subprocess, sys

def validate_az_commands(commands: list[str]) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Verify that a list of az CLI command prefixes exist in the installed CLI.
    Catches cases where a command was renamed or moved to an extension.
    """
    issues = []
    for cmd in commands:
        result = subprocess.run(
            f"az {cmd} --help",
            shell=True,
            capture_output=True,
            text=True,
        )
        if result.returncode != 0 or "ERROR" in result.stderr:
            issues.append({
                "command": f"az {cmd}",
                "error": result.stderr.strip()[:100],
            })
    return issues

# Commands used in our runbooks — validate these pass in CI
RUNBOOK_COMMANDS = [
    "webapp show",
    "webapp log config",
    "monitor metrics list",
    "network watcher test-connectivity",
    "aks get-credentials",
    "containerapp show",
]

issues = validate_az_commands(RUNBOOK_COMMANDS)
if issues:
    for i in issues:
        print(f"[FAIL] {i['command']}: {i['error']}")
    sys.exit(1)
else:
    print(f"All {len(RUNBOOK_COMMANDS)} runbook commands validated.")
Keeping Up with Azure Documentation Updates
Azure documentation is updated daily. The following approaches help teams stay current with changes that affect their deployed services.
Exact documentation changes at file level
Product team posts, preview announcements, deep dives
RSS feeds on learn.microsoft.com per service
What's new pages per product area

import feedparser  # pip install feedparser

def check_azure_updates(max_items: int = 10) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Fetch the latest Azure Updates from the official RSS feed.
    Use in a scheduled job to surface relevant service changes to your team.
    """
    feed = feedparser.parse("https://azure.microsoft.com/updates/feed/")
    return [
        {
            "title": entry.title,
            "published": entry.get("published", ""),
            "link": entry.link,
            "tags": [t.term for t in entry.get("tags", [])],
        }
        for entry in feed.entries[:max_items]
    ]

# Filter to updates relevant to your deployed services
SERVICE_KEYWORDS = ["app service", "aks", "azure sql", "key vault", "container apps"]

updates = check_azure_updates(max_items=50)
relevant = [
    u for u in updates
    if any(kw in u["title"].lower() for kw in SERVICE_KEYWORDS)
]

print(f"Relevant Azure updates ({len(relevant)}/{len(updates)}):")
for u in relevant[:5]:
    print(f"  [{u['published'][:10]}] {u['title']}")
    print(f"    {u['link']}")

For environments without reliable internet access (air-gapped labs, secure facilities), downloading documentation for offline use is practical. The Azure Docs GitHub repository is the canonical source.

# Clone the full Azure docs repository (warning: ~2 GB)
git clone --depth=1 https://github.com/MicrosoftDocs/azure-docs.git

# Search offline with grep
grep -r "api-version=2022" azure-docs/articles/app-service/ --include="*.md" -l

# Build a local search index with ripgrep
cd azure-docs
rg "Container Apps.*vCPU" articles/ --type md -C 2

# Use mdbook to build a local HTML site from specific chapters
# cargo install mdbook
cat > book.toml << 'EOF'
[book]
title = "Azure Docs Local"
src = "articles/app-service"
EOF
mdbook build  # generates ./book/html/ — serve with any static file server
python3 -m http.server 8080 --directory book/html

Azure documentation at learn.microsoft.com/azure is organised into conceptual guides, quickstarts, how-to guides, REST API reference, CLI reference, SDK reference, the Architecture Center, and the Well-Architected Framework.
Use the REST API reference as the authoritative source for request/response schemas and supported API versions — cross-validate with the api-version registered in the resource provider.
The --debug flag on any az command shows the underlying REST calls, bridging CLI usage with API documentation.
Azure-Samples on GitHub provides complete working applications per use-case — prefer these over code snippets when learning a service integration pattern.
Architecture Center reference architectures are validated end-to-end designs; use them as the starting point for production system design.
All documentation is open-source on GitHub — submit pull requests to fix errors or improve examples directly.
